summary:
# Verification Report

**Verdict:** FAIL
**Claims reviewed:** 22
**Sources reviewed:** 16
**Open agenda items:** 6

## Findings
1. [HIGH] US and Israeli airstrikes on Iran began February 28, 2026, constituting the largest US military operation since the 2003 Iraq invasion. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
2. [HIGH] Brent crude surged ~13% intraday to $83/bbl and WTI rose to $76/bbl following the strikes, from a pre-war Brent price of ~$72.48. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
3. [HIGH] Global oil prices surged more than 25% since the start of the war; one source (Star Tribune) cites +35% in the first week. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
4. [HIGH] Approximately 20 million barrels per day flowed through the Strait of Hormuz in 2025; this has slowed 'to a trickle' per the IEA since conflict began. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
5. [HIGH] About 140 million barrels of oil (≈1.4 days of global demand) from Saudi Arabia, UAE, Iraq, Kuwait, and Qatar are frozen in ports due to the Strait near-closure. — Core claim lacks two independent tier 1-3 sources. Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
6. [HIGH] S&P 500 fell ~2% in the first 5 trading days after strikes, vs. –7% for European equities and –8.4% for MSCI Emerging Markets. — Core claim lacks two independent tier 1-3 sources. Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
7. [MEDIUM] S&P 500 traded at 6,683 on March 12, 2026, down 2.5% YTD with RSI near oversold at 35.9; 200-day SMA support at ~6,601. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
8. [HIGH] February 2026 CPI came in at 2.4% YoY (+0.3% MoM); core CPI +0.2% MoM — in line with expectations, before the full energy shock was captured. — Core claim lacks two independent tier 1-3 sources. Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
9. [HIGH] US gasoline prices rose more than $0.50/gallon since the conflict began, contributing to consumer inflation pressure on top of a 26% cumulative price increase since 2020. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
10. [MEDIUM] FOMC rate-cut probability for March 2026 is 4.7% per CME FedWatch; rate cuts pushed back to September 2026. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
11. [HIGH] Expert Robert McNally (energy strategist) stated: 'A prolonged closure of the Strait of Hormuz is a guaranteed global recession.' — Core claim lacks two independent tier 1-3 sources. Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
12. [MEDIUM] Israeli strikes on Iran on June 13, 2025 caused an 8.5% oil price spike that quickly reversed, contrasting with the sustained 25%+ move in 2026. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
13. [MEDIUM] In 2026, US small/mid-caps (+7–12% YTD), international equities (+11% YTD), and bonds (+1.5% Feb) significantly outperformed the S&P 500 (–2.5% YTD), representing a major market rotation. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
14. [MEDIUM] Three-quarters of Strait of Hormuz crude goes to China, India, Japan, and South Korea; China receives half its crude imports via the Strait. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
15. [HIGH] The +13% figure (Thomson Reuters) is a Day-1 intraday surge (Brent $72.80 → $82.37 on March 2); the +35% figure (Star Tribune) measures cumulative gain from January 1 2026 low (~$61) to end of Week 1 (~$82-85), roughly 30-35%. Both are correct; they measure different time windows. — Core claim lacks two independent tier 1-3 sources. Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
16. [HIGH] Brent crude peaked at ~$119/bbl on March 9, 2026 — a +63% surge from the pre-war close of ~$72.80 in just 9 trading days, the fastest major oil price spike in modern history. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
17. [HIGH] Saudi Petroline has design capacity of 5-7 mbd and is ramping toward full utilization; UAE ADCOP has 1.5-1.8 mbd capacity with ~440k bpd spare at current 71% utilization. Combined bypass potential: ~5.5-6.8 mbd. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
18. [HIGH] Hormuz normally carries ~20 mbd of crude and LNG. Pipeline bypass capacity (3.5-5.5 mbd incremental) covers at most 17-28% of normal Hormuz throughput, leaving a structural shortfall of 12-17 mbd if fully closed. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
19. [HIGH] OPEC+ nominal spare capacity is ~4.1 mbd (mostly Saudi/UAE), but the actual production increase agreed March 1, 2026 was only +206,000 bpd — 5% of theoretical spare, a symbolically inadequate response. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
20. [MEDIUM] The 2026 US equity market rotation (underperformance vs. international/small-cap) is approximately 60% pre-war structural (dollar cycle, tariff drag, fiscal uncertainty) and 40% war-accelerated (oil shock amplifying US earnings headwinds). Brent was already +30% YTD before the conflict's peak. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
21. [HIGH] Brent fell 12.6% in a single session on March 10 after Trump's de-escalation comments, then partially recovered as war escalation continued, demonstrating extreme political-driven volatility. — Core claim lacks two independent tier 1-3 sources. Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
22. [MEDIUM] Analysts project Brent in the $110-130/bbl range over the next 3 months if Hormuz disruption persists, with $120+ representing demand-destruction territory. — Quant claim lacks dataset provenance or generated charts.
